title: Your brain sees faces in coffee foam — on purpose
author: Popkin
summary:
This cute photo collection shows different cups of coffee that look like they have faces in their bubbles. This isn't human-made latte art, though. These coffee cups just happen to come with a personality. I'd consider it to be good luck if my morning coffee was smiling at me like this. — Read the rest

The post
full_text:
This cute photo collection shows different cups of coffee that look like they have faces in their bubbles. This isn't human-made latte art, though. These coffee cups just happen to come with a personality. I'd consider it to be good luck if my morning coffee was smiling at me like this.

From Visual Spirits:

"Humans are incredibly good at spotting faces, so good that we often see them in random places. This is called pareidolia, and it happens because the brain would rather mistake something for a face than miss one entirely.

Deep in your brain, the fusiform face area is always on alert, scanning for eyes, expressions, and familiar patterns. It works so fast that even the simplest shapes, like two dots and a line, can instantly feel like a face looking back at you. That is why a swirl of coffee foam can seem surprising, or a cloud can look like it is smiling. It is not the object that changed. It is your brain doing exactly what it was designed to do, turning randomness into something recognizable."
